questions.q: How was the existence of antimatter (the anti-electron) first arrived at?
options: It was discovered by accident in a cloud chamber before any theory predicted it
Dirac predicted it purely from the mathematics of his relativistic equation for the electron, before it was ever observed
It was created deliberately in a particle accelerator
Einstein predicted it from \( E = mc^2 \)
questions.explanation: Dirac's relativistic equation for the electron had negative-energy solutions he interpreted as a particle with the electron's mass but opposite charge — the anti-electron. He predicted it from pure mathematics in 1931, before Anderson observed it in 1932.

questions.q: How did Carl Anderson identify the positron in his cloud chamber in 1932?
options: It left a much heavier, denser track than an electron
It had the mass of an electron but curved the opposite way in the magnetic field, showing a positive charge
It left no track at all but emitted gamma rays
It travelled faster than the speed of light
questions.explanation: The track had an electron's mass (same wispy, low-density trail) but curved the opposite way in the magnetic field, meaning a positive charge. A lead plate let Anderson confirm the direction of travel. A particle as light as an electron but positively charged was Dirac's anti-electron — the positron.

questions.q: What are the key properties of the positron relative to the electron?
options: Same charge, much greater mass
Same mass as the electron, but the opposite (positive) charge
Greater mass and the opposite charge
Zero mass and zero charge
questions.explanation: The positron is the electron's antimatter twin: identical mass (rest energy 0.511 MeV) but opposite charge (+1 instead of -1). This is Dirac's symmetry between matter and antimatter.

questions.q: When an electron and positron annihilate, what is the total energy released, and how is it carried away?
options: 0.511 MeV, carried by a single gamma photon
1.022 MeV, carried by two 511 keV gamma photons emitted back to back
1.022 MeV, carried by one high-energy photon
0.511 MeV, carried by an electron and a neutrino
questions.explanation: Each particle has rest energy 0.511 MeV, so total energy released is \(2 \times 0.511 = 1.022\ \mathrm{MeV}\), shared equally between two gamma photons of 511 keV each. They are emitted back to back (180 degrees apart).

questions.q: Why are exactly TWO gamma rays produced, flying off in opposite directions, rather than one?
options: Because the electron and positron each become a photon
Because the pair is essentially at rest (total momentum ≈ 0), so conservation of momentum requires two photons with equal and opposite momenta
Because gamma rays always travel in pairs
Because charge conservation requires two photons
questions.explanation: Just before annihilation the pair is nearly at rest, so total momentum is ~0. Momentum must be conserved, so the photons must carry equal and opposite momenta — two photons back to back. A single photon could never have zero momentum, so one photon is impossible.

questions.q: Fluorine-18 (used in PET) decays by beta-plus decay. Which force drives this, and what happens to the nucleus?
options: The strong nuclear force; a neutron turns into a proton
The weak nuclear force; a proton turns into a neutron, emitting a positron and a neutrino
The electromagnetic force; an electron is emitted
Gravity; the nucleus splits in two
questions.explanation: Beta-plus decay is driven by the weak nuclear force — the only force that changes one particle type into another this way. A proton becomes a neutron, emitting a positron and a neutrino: \({}^{18}_{9}\mathrm{F} \to {}^{18}_{8}\mathrm{O} + e^+ + \nu\). The atomic number drops by one; the mass number is unchanged.

questions.q: Why do tumours show up as bright hotspots on a PET scan using FDG (radioactive glucose)?
options: Tumours emit their own gamma rays without any tracer
Cancer cells consume glucose far faster than normal tissue (the Warburg effect), so they take up and trap more FDG, producing more annihilations there
Tumours repel the positrons, concentrating them at the edges
FDG is attracted to tumours by magnetism
questions.explanation: Cancer cells are glucose gluttons (the Warburg effect), so they take up the most FDG, which then gets phosphorylated and trapped inside the cell. More trapped fluorine-18 means more positron emission and more annihilations, making tumours glowing hotspots on the reconstructed map.

questions.q: What does the PET scanner's ring of detectors actually detect, and how does a single 'coincidence' help locate an annihilation?
options: It detects positrons directly as they leave the body
It detects pairs of 511 keV gamma rays arriving at opposite detectors near-simultaneously; the annihilation lies on the line joining them (a line of response)
It detects the fluorine-18 nuclei using magnetism
It detects the neutrinos emitted in beta-plus decay
questions.explanation: The positrons annihilate within a millimetre or two inside the body; the scanner never sees them. It detects the two back-to-back 511 keV gamma rays. Two opposite detectors firing within nanoseconds define a line of response — the annihilation happened somewhere on that line. Millions of crossing lines reconstruct the 3D map.

questions.q: Fluorine-18 has a half-life of about 110 minutes. Approximately what fraction of the original activity remains after 6 half-lives (~11 hours)?
options: About 50%
About 25%
About 1.6%
About 0%
questions.explanation: After \(n\) half-lives the fraction remaining is \((\tfrac{1}{2})^n\). After 6 half-lives: \((\tfrac{1}{2})^6 = \tfrac{1}{64} \approx 1.6\%\). This is why the patient is essentially non-radioactive by the next day, while ~110 min is still long enough to ship the tracer and let cells take it up.

questions.q: Which conservation laws set the two key features used by a PET scanner — the photon energy and their direction?
options: Conservation of mass sets both
Conservation of energy fixes each photon at 511 keV; conservation of momentum fixes them flying back to back
Conservation of charge fixes the energy; conservation of mass fixes the direction
Conservation of momentum fixes the energy; conservation of energy fixes the direction
questions.explanation: Energy conservation gives each photon a sharp, recognisable 511 keV (half of 1.022 MeV). Momentum conservation, from a near-stationary pair, forces the two photons to fly out in exactly opposite directions. Together these give the scanner its lines of response. Charge is also conserved (0 before, 0 after).
